basmala: In the name of God, the All-Merciful, the Compassionate.
intro: Al-Fatḥ — “The Victory,” or rather “The Opening” — was revealed on the return from Ḥudaybiya, that seemingly bitter treaty which God nonetheless calls “a manifest victory” (fatḥ mubīn). The sūra unfolds the arc of a triumph not measured by arms: forgiveness of faults, the perfecting of grace, mighty help, and above all the sakīna — the serenity — that God sends down into the hearts of the believers so that their faith may grow faith upon faith. At its center, the Pledge of Good Pleasure under the tree (bayʿat al-riḍwān), where “the hand of God is above their hands.” It is sealed on the portrait of the companions — firm against denial, tender among themselves, marked on the brow by prostration — likened, in the Torah and the Gospel, to the seed that puts forth its shoot, strengthens, and stands firm upon its stalk. Qushayrī makes of the fatḥ an inner opening: God unlocks for the Prophet what remained closed to the heart of every other, and of the sakīna the repose of the inmost self in God, by which the heart rises from reflection to the spirit of certainty, and the sciences become necessary.
themes: the manifest victory / the inner opening
the Pledge of Good Pleasure under the tree
the hand of God above the hands
the portrait of the companions and the seed

versets.ar: بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا
versets.tr: innā fataḥnā laka fatḥan mubīnan
versets.trad: Truly We have granted you a manifest victory,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: We have decreed in your favor a manifest judgment, We have decreed the strengthening of the religion of Islam and victory over your enemy. Yet the victory (fatḥ) is not merely outward: We have honored you by opening what remained closed to the heart of all others before you — through the unfolding of Islam's laws and through the inner openings of your blessed heart. The verse was revealed concerning Ḥudaybiya, an opening without battle; and it is also said: “We have guided you to the ways of Islam and made the affairs of religion easy for you.”

mots.def: fataḥa: to open, conquer, grant victory.

mots.racine: ف ت ح / ب ي ن

versets.ar: لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا
versets.tr: li-yaghfira laka llāhu mā taqaddama min dhanbika wa-mā taʾakhkhara wa-yutimma niʿmatahu ʿalayka wa-yahdiyaka ṣirāṭan mustaqīman
versets.trad: that God may forgive you your sins, the former and the latter, perfect His grace upon you, and guide you on a straight path,
versets.commentaire: Since the prophets are preserved from sin, Qushayrī reports that the “former” and the “latter” refer to what preceded prophethood, or are read as the leaving of the more perfect (tark al-awlā) rather than as sin. He perfects His grace upon you through prophethood, through a goodly end, through the spreading of the Law, through intercession for your community, and through the vision of God on the promised Day. And “He guides you on a straight path”: He keeps you firm upon it, adds for you guidance upon guidance, and through you guides creation to the Real. It is also said: He guides you by your leaving of your own portion.

mots.racine: ق د م / أ خ ر
ت م م / ن ع م
ص ر ط / ق و م

versets.ar: وَيَنصُرَكَ اللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا
versets.tr: wa-yanṣuraka llāhu naṣran ʿazīzan
versets.trad: and that God may grant you a mighty help.
versets.commentaire: A help “without abasement,” says Qushayrī, in which you are victorious while none overcomes you. But the highest help is inward: He helps you against your passion and your soul, and helps you through the beauty of your character and the endurance of your people's offenses. Thus this verse gathers several favors with which God honored His Prophet: victory over soul and enemy, the easing of what remained closed to others, forgiveness, the perfecting of grace, guidance, and help.

mots.racine: ن ص ر / ع ز ز

versets.ar: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ لِيَزْدَادُوا إِيمَانًا مَّعَ إِيمَانِهِمْ وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا
versets.tr: huwa lladhī anzala s-sakīnata fī qulūbi l-muʾminīna li-yazdādū īmānan maʿa īmānihim wa-lillāhi junūdu s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-kāna llāhu ʿalīman ḥakīman
versets.trad: It is He who sent down serenity (sakīna) into the hearts of the believers, that their faith might increase ever further — to God belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth, and God is all-knowing, wise,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī distinguishes two degrees of the sakīna. For the generality of believers it is repose, tranquility and certitude. But for the elect, the sakīna is that in which the heart finds rest, among the insights and the proofs; by it the heart rises from the realm of reflection to the spirit of certainty and the coolness of the inmost self, and the sciences become necessary. “That their faith might increase”: certainty upon their certainty, repose upon their repose — the moons of the eye of certainty rise over the stars of the knowledge of certainty, then the sun of the truth of certainty rises over the full moon of the eye of certainty. And “to God belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth”: whatever the Real sends upon a thing is among His hosts, whether upon His friend in ease and trial, or upon His enemy.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / س ك ن
mots.def: sakīna: tranquility, inner peace sent down by God.

mots.racine: ق ل ب / أ م ن
mots.sens: into the hearts of the believers

mots.racine: ز ي د / أ م ن

mots.sens: the hosts of the heavens and the earth

versets.ar: لِّيُدْخِلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا وَيُكَفِّرَ عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عِندَ اللَّهِ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا
versets.tr: li-yudkhila l-muʾminīna wa-l-muʾmināti jannātin tajrī min taḥtihā l-anhāru khālidīna fīhā wa-yukaffira ʿanhum sayyiʾātihim wa-kāna dhālika ʿinda llāhi fawzan ʿaẓīman
versets.trad: that He may admit the believing men and the believing women into Gardens beneath which rivers flow, abiding therein forever, and absolve them of their evil deeds. That, with God, is a supreme triumph.
versets.commentaire: He covers their sins and effaces them — and that is the supreme triumph, the attaining of the desired object. Qushayrī notes that each one's request, his hope, his aim and his intention differ; yet to all He has promised they would attain it. Each receives, in the Garden, that toward which the secret of his heart was straining.

mots.racine: ج ن ن / ن ه ر
ك ف ر / س و أ
mots.sens: and absolve them of their evil deeds

mots.racine: ف و ز / ع ظ م

versets.ar: وَيُعَذِّبَ الْمُنَافِقِينَ وَالْمُنَافِقَاتِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ وَالْمُشْرِكَاتِ الظَّانِّينَ بِاللَّهِ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ عَلَيْهِمْ دَائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ وَغَضِبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَعَنَهُمْ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ وَسَاءَتْ مَصِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-yuʿadhdhiba l-munāfiqīna wa-l-munāfiqāti wa-l-mushrikīna wa-l-mushrikāti ẓ-ẓānnīna bi-llāhi ẓanna s-sawʾi ʿalayhim dāʾiratu s-sawʾi wa-ghaḍiba llāhu ʿalayhim wa-laʿanahum wa-aʿadda lahum jahannama wa-sāʾat maṣīran
versets.trad: and that He may punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the idolaters, men and women, who harbor evil thoughts of God. Upon them an evil turn! God is angry with them, He has cursed them and prepared for them Hell. A wretched destination!
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī defines the “evil thought”: it is to suppose, without warrant, that God will not aid His religion nor His Prophet. “Upon them the evil turn”: its consequences revolve back upon them and encompass them. And the curse means that He removes them from His bounty, that His word is realized against them, and that the prior decree is fulfilled in them. Doubt of God's faithfulness is the secret spring of all hypocrisy.

mots.racine: ن ف ق / ش ر ك
ظ ن ن / س و أ
د و ر / س و أ
mots.def: dāʾira: a circle, a turn that comes back round.

versets.ar: وَلِلَّهِ جُنُودُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-lillāhi junūdu s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi wa-kāna llāhu ʿazīzan ḥakīman
versets.trad: To God belong the hosts of the heavens and the earth. God is mighty, wise!
versets.commentaire: The refrain of the hosts returns, but sealed this time with ʿazīz (the Mighty) and ḥakīm (the Wise). Every force exerted in the heavens and the earth belongs to Him: none is mighty save by His might, none triumphs save by His decree. The repetition steadies the believer's heart: the troops of the Real have no need of the number of men.

mots.sens: the hosts of the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: ع ز ز / ح ك م

versets.ar: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ شَاهِدًا وَمُبَشِّرًا وَنَذِيرًا
versets.tr: innā arsalnāka shāhidan wa-mubashshiran wa-nadhīran
versets.trad: Truly We have sent you as a witness, a bearer of good tidings, and a warner,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī unfolds the senses of “witness” (shāhid): a witness over your community on the Day of Resurrection; a witness over the messengers and the Books; a witness to Our oneness and Our lordship; a witness for your community of our profession of unity. The bearer of tidings brings them the good news of reward, while the warner turns creatures away from disobedience. It is also said: a witness from Us, a herald by Our command, a warner from before Us, for Us and from Us.

versets.ar: لِّتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُعَزِّرُوهُ وَتُوَقِّرُوهُ وَتُسَبِّحُوهُ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلًا
versets.tr: li-tuʾminū bi-llāhi wa-rasūlihi wa-tuʿazzirūhu wa-tuwaqqirūhu wa-tusabbiḥūhu bukratan wa-aṣīlan
versets.trad: that you may believe in God and His Messenger, and support him, and revere him, and glorify Him at dawn and at dusk.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī specifies the last two verbs by referring them to the Prophet: to support him (taʿzīr) is to prefer him to yourself in every way and to set his judgment before yours; to revere him (tawqīr) is to follow his sunna, knowing that he is the master of creatures. As for the glorifying, it returns to God: to extol Him and hold Him above all, at morning and at evening.

mots.racine: ب ك ر / أ ص ل

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُبَايِعُونَكَ إِنَّمَا يُبَايِعُونَ اللَّهَ يَدُ اللَّهِ فَوْقَ أَيْدِيهِمْ فَمَن نَّكَثَ فَإِنَّمَا يَنكُثُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَوْفَىٰ بِمَا عَاهَدَ عَلَيْهُ اللَّهَ فَسَيُؤْتِيهِ أَجْرًا عَظِيمًا
versets.tr: inna lladhīna yubāyiʿūnaka innamā yubāyiʿūna llāha yadu llāhi fawqa aydīhim fa-man nakatha fa-innamā yankuthu ʿalā nafsihi wa-man awfā bi-mā ʿāhada ʿalayhu llāha fa-sa-yuʾtīhi ajran ʿaẓīman
versets.trad: Truly those who pledge allegiance to you pledge allegiance only to God. The hand of God is above their hands. Whoever breaks his pledge breaks it against himself, and whoever is faithful to what he has covenanted with God, He will grant him a mighty reward.
versets.commentaire: This is the Pledge of Good Pleasure (bayʿat al-riḍwān), made under the tree at Ḥudaybiya. Qushayrī interprets “the hand of God is above their hands” without anthropomorphism: it is God's favor upon them through enabling grace and guidance, above their hands stretched out in fidelity; it is the power of God in the aid of His religion, higher than their aid. He sees in it an explicit declaration of the “oneness of the act” (ʿayn al-jamʿ), as in: “It was not you who threw when you threw, but God threw.” When the servant acts with God in sincerity, the apparent intermediaries are effaced in his secret, and the decree returns to the One.

mots.def: bāyaʿa: to conclude a pledge of allegiance.

mots.sens: the hand of God is above their hands
mots.def: yad: hand (here a metaphor of favor and power).
nakatha: to betray, break a pledge.

versets.ar: سَيَقُولُ لَكَ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ مِنَ الْأَعْرَابِ شَغَلَتْنَا أَمْوَالُنَا وَأَهْلُونَا فَاسْتَغْفِرْ لَنَا يَقُولُونَ بِأَلْسِنَتِهِم مَّا لَيْسَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ لَكُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا إِنْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ ضَرًّا أَوْ أَرَادَ بِكُمْ نَفْعًا بَلْ كَانَ اللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرًا
versets.tr: sa-yaqūlu laka l-mukhallafūna mina l-aʿrābi shaghalatnā amwālunā wa-ahlūnā fa-staghfir lanā yaqūlūna bi-alsinatihim mā laysa fī qulūbihim qul fa-man yamliku lakum mina llāhi shayʾan in arāda bikum ḍarran aw arāda bikum nafʿan bal kāna llāhu bi-mā taʿmalūna khabīran
versets.trad: The Bedouins who stayed behind will say to you: “Our wealth and our families kept us occupied; ask forgiveness for us!” They say with their tongues what is not in their hearts. Say: “Who then can avail you anything against God, should He intend you harm or intend you benefit?” Indeed God is fully aware of what you do.
versets.commentaire: Bedouins held back from setting out for Ḥudaybiya, claiming that wealth and families had detained them, then returned with excuses and a plea for intercession. God exposed their falsehood: intercession is indifferent to them, for they say with their mouths what is not in their hearts. Qushayrī draws two maxims: whatever turns the servant away from God is an ill omen for him, and the excuse of the lukewarm and the repentance of the hypocrite are, neither of them, true realities.

mots.racine: ش غ ل / م و ل

mots.def: khabīr: who knows the depths of things.

versets.ar: بَلْ ظَنَنتُمْ أَن لَّن يَنقَلِبَ الرَّسُولُ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَىٰ أَهْلِيهِمْ أَبَدًا وَزُيِّنَ ذَٰلِكَ فِي قُلُوبِكُمْ وَظَنَنتُمْ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ وَكُنتُمْ قَوْمًا بُورًا
versets.tr: bal ẓanantum an lan yanqaliba r-rasūlu wa-l-muʾminūna ilā ahlīhim abadan wa-zuyyina dhālika fī qulūbikum wa-ẓanantum ẓanna s-sawʾi wa-kuntum qawman būran
versets.trad: Indeed you thought that the Messenger and the believers would never return to their families, and that was made fair-seeming in your hearts; you thought an evil thought, and you became a people doomed.
versets.commentaire: You imagined the Messenger and the believers would never return, and this deceptive wish was made fair in your hearts: you became a people destined for ruin and corruption. Qushayrī observes that the enemy, when he cannot harm with his hand, desires what lies beyond his power — and this is the trait of every impotent and base soul. Worse still: among His severe punishments, God lets the liar picture a thing he covets until he believes it has come to pass — then He torments him by withholding it.

mots.def: zuyyina: to be adorned, made attractive.

versets.ar: وَمَن لَّمْ يُؤْمِن بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ فَإِنَّا أَعْتَدْنَا لِلْكَافِرِينَ سَعِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-man lam yuʾmin bi-llāhi wa-rasūlihi fa-innā aʿtadnā li-l-kāfirīna saʿīran
versets.trad: Whoever does not believe in God and His Messenger — We have prepared for the disbelievers a blaze.
versets.commentaire: What is to come is near, says Qushayrī: God loosens the rein for the wrongdoers, but they do not escape His punishment. And he presses further: in truth, what proceeds from them is what He brings to pass upon them — their very deeds fall under the decree. The blaze is but the return, upon them, of what they have knotted.

versets.ar: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَاءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَاءُ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-lillāhi mulku s-samāwāti wa-l-arḍi yaghfiru li-man yashāʾu wa-yuʿadhdhibu man yashāʾu wa-kāna llāhu ghafūran raḥīman
versets.trad: To God belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills. God is forgiving, compassionate.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī underscores the absolute freedom of the decree: He forgives — and no partner says to Him, “do not”; He punishes whom He wills — and no hindrance opposes His act to say, “do not.” Since the kingdom of the heavens and the earth belongs to Him alone, no recourse, no intercessor holds outside His will; and yet He names Himself, here itself, Forgiving and Compassionate.

mots.sens: the dominion of the heavens and the earth

mots.racine: غ ف ر / ش ي أ
غ ف ر / ر ح م

versets.ar: سَيَقُولُ الْمُخَلَّفُونَ إِذَا انطَلَقْتُمْ إِلَىٰ مَغَانِمَ لِتَأْخُذُوهَا ذَرُونَا نَتَّبِعْكُمْ يُرِيدُونَ أَن يُبَدِّلُوا كَلَامَ اللَّهِ قُل لَّن تَتَّبِعُونَا كَذَٰلِكُمْ قَالَ اللَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ فَسَيَقُولُونَ بَلْ تَحْسُدُونَنَا بَلْ كَانُوا لَا يَفْقَهُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا
versets.tr: sa-yaqūlu l-mukhallafūna idhā nṭalaqtum ilā maghānima li-taʾkhudhūhā dharūnā nattabiʿkum yurīdūna an yubaddilū kalāma llāhi qul lan tattabiʿūnā kadhālikum qāla llāhu min qablu fa-sa-yaqūlūna bal taḥsudūnanā bal kānū lā yafqahūna illā qalīlan
versets.trad: Those who stayed behind will say, when you set out toward the spoils to seize them: “Let us follow you!” They wish to alter the word of God. Say: “You shall not follow us; thus God said beforehand.” Then they will say: “Rather, you envy us.” No — they understand but little.
versets.commentaire: After Ḥudaybiya, God promised Khaybar and its spoils. The stragglers then wished to follow, lured by gain; the Prophet replied that only he who set out for Ḥudaybiya shall set out for Khaybar, by God's decree. They accused the believers of envy. Qushayrī notes their end: people of greed, they did not attain their craving, were turned back in humiliation, and their affair was exposed.

mots.racine: ط ل ق / غ ن م
mots.sens: when you set out toward spoils
mots.def: maghānim: spoils; inṭalaqa: to set out.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / ت ب ع
mots.def: dhara: to leave; ittabaʿa: to follow.

mots.racine: ب د ل / ك ل م
mots.sens: to alter the word of God

versets.ar: قُل لِّلْمُخَلَّفِينَ مِنَ الْأَعْرَابِ سَتُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ أُولِي بَأْسٍ شَدِيدٍ تُقَاتِلُونَهُمْ أَوْ يُسْلِمُونَ فَإِن تُطِيعُوا يُؤْتِكُمُ اللَّهُ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا وَإِن تَتَوَلَّوْا كَمَا تَوَلَّيْتُم مِّن قَبْلُ يُعَذِّبْكُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: qul li-l-mukhallafīna mina l-aʿrābi sa-tudʿawna ilā qawmin ulī baʾsin shadīdin tuqātilūnahum aw yuslimūna fa-in tuṭīʿū yuʾtikumu llāhu ajran ḥasanan wa-in tatawallaw kamā tawallaytum min qablu yuʿadhdhibkum ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: Say to the Bedouins who stayed behind: “You will be called against a people of great might, whom you will fight unless they submit. If you obey, God will give you a fair reward; but if you turn away as you turned away before, He will punish you with a painful punishment.”
versets.commentaire: A second chance is offered them: a future summons against a formidable people. Qushayrī draws hope from this: the verse shows that the servant may have an unapproved beginning, then change afterward toward righteousness — as was their case. He cites the line: “When a man becomes corrupt after his uprightness, hope still for him the return of uprightness… perhaps.”

mots.racine: ب أ س / ش د د
ع ذ ب / أ ل م

versets.ar: لَّيْسَ عَلَى الْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى الْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى الْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ وَمَن يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِن تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهَارُ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ يُعَذِّبْهُ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: laysa ʿalā l-aʿmā ḥarajun wa-lā ʿalā l-aʿraji ḥarajun wa-lā ʿalā l-marīḍi ḥarajun wa-man yuṭiʿi llāha wa-rasūlahu yudkhilhu jannātin tajrī min taḥtihā l-anhāru wa-man yatawalla yuʿadhdhibhu ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: There is no blame upon the blind, nor upon the lame, nor upon the sick. Whoever obeys God and His Messenger, He will admit him into Gardens beneath which rivers flow; but whoever turns away, He will punish with a painful punishment.
versets.commentaire: These are “the people of excuse”: God lifts the constraint from those who, prevented, abstain from battle. Qushayrī widens the dispensation to the inner field: likewise, whoever has an excuse in the struggle against the soul — God loves that His concessions (rukhaṣ) be taken up, just as He loves that His firm obligations (ʿazāʾim) be fulfilled. A rare touch in him, who usually presses toward the rigor of resolve.

mots.racine: ع م ي / ع ر ج / م ر ض
mots.sens: the blind, the lame, the sick

versets.ar: لَّقَدْ رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ فَعَلِمَ مَا فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ فَأَنزَلَ السَّكِينَةَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَثَابَهُمْ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا
versets.tr: la-qad raḍiya llāhu ʿani l-muʾminīna idh yubāyiʿūnaka taḥta sh-shajarati fa-ʿalima mā fī qulūbihim fa-anzala s-sakīnata ʿalayhim wa-athābahum fatḥan qarīban
versets.trad: God was well pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree. He knew what was in their hearts, so He sent down serenity upon them and rewarded them with a near victory
versets.commentaire: This is the Pledge of Good Pleasure (bayʿat al-riḍwān), under the tree at Ḥudaybiya — so named from this very verse: “God was well pleased with the believers.” Qushayrī recalls that, entry to Mecca having been barred that year, a disturbance brushed certain hearts; but God knew the disquiet and doubt that stirred in them, sent down the sakīna upon them, and steadied them with certainty. From this he draws that troubling thoughts may cross a man and cast him into doubt — without one needing to dwell on them: for when God wills good for a servant, He makes oneness the companion of his heart and realization the companion of his secret, so that the devil's guile does him no harm.

mots.def: raḍiya: to be pleased, to accept.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / س ك ن
ف ت ح / ق ر ب

versets.ar: وَمَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً يَأْخُذُونَهَا وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزًا حَكِيمًا
versets.tr: wa-maghānima kathīratan yaʾkhudhūnahā wa-kāna llāhu ʿazīzan ḥakīman
versets.trad: and with abundant spoils which they would take. God is mighty, wise.
versets.commentaire: The “abundant spoils” are those of Khaybar, obtained shortly after; it is also said: all that the Muslims will gain until the Day of Resurrection. Qushayrī seals it with the two Names: ʿazīz, whose might bestows without resistance, and ḥakīm, whose wisdom measures each gift to its hour.

mots.racine: غ ن م / ك ث ر
ع ز ز / ح ك م

versets.ar: وَعَدَكُمُ اللَّهُ مَغَانِمَ كَثِيرَةً تَأْخُذُونَهَا فَعَجَّلَ لَكُمْ هَٰذِهِ وَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَ النَّاسِ عَنكُمْ وَلِتَكُونَ آيَةً لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَيَهْدِيَكُمْ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا
versets.tr: waʿadakumu llāhu maghānima kathīratan taʾkhudhūnahā fa-ʿajjala lakum hādhihi wa-kaffa aydiya n-nāsi ʿankum wa-li-takūna āyatan li-l-muʾminīna wa-yahdiyakum ṣirāṭan mustaqīman
versets.trad: God has promised you abundant spoils which you will take, and He has hastened this for you, and restrained the hands of the people from you — that it may be a sign for the believers, and that He may guide you to a straight path.
versets.commentaire: God hastened these spoils for them and “restrained the hands of the people from you.” Qushayrī reads in it the divine guarding: when they left Medina, God protected them, watched over their families, and defended their hearth. And inwardly, to “restrain the hands of the people” from the servant is that He provide for him from where he does not reckon, so that he need not stretch out his hand to men, and that He keep the hands of oppressors far from him. All this so that it may be “a sign”: a mark on which they lean to recognize God's protection, and a support for trust.

mots.racine: ك ف ف / ي د ي
mots.sens: He restrained the hands of the people

mots.racine: أ ي ي / أ م ن

versets.ar: وَأُخْرَىٰ لَمْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهَا قَدْ أَحَاطَ اللَّهُ بِهَا وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-ukhrā lam taqdirū ʿalayhā qad aḥāṭa llāhu bihā wa-kāna llāhu ʿalā kulli shayʾin qadīran
versets.trad: And other [gains] you were not able to attain — God has already encompassed them. God is powerful over all things.
versets.commentaire: “And another [victory] you could not attain, God has already encompassed.” It is seen as the conquest of the Byzantines and Persians, or of Mecca. Qushayrī draws the lesson of the closing verse: God is powerful over all things — so attach your hearts to none but Him. What lies beyond your power, His knowledge has already enveloped.

versets.ar: وَلَوْ قَاتَلَكُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوَلَّوُا الْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-law qātalakumu lladhīna kafarū la-wallawu l-adbāra thumma lā yajidūna waliyyan wa-lā naṣīran
versets.trad: Had the disbelievers fought you, they would have turned their backs in flight, then found neither protector nor helper.
versets.commentaire: Had they fought — Khaybar, Asad, Ghaṭafān and the rest — they would have turned their backs and found, apart from God, no helper. Qushayrī lets the meaning resound: whoever rises against the divine decree dooms himself to rout, for no protector stands before the One who withdraws His support.

mots.racine: و ل ي / د ب ر
و ل ي / ن ص ر

versets.ar: سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّةِ اللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا
versets.tr: sunnata llāhi llatī qad khalat min qablu wa-lan tajida li-sunnati llāhi tabdīlan
versets.trad: Such is the way of God that has passed before; and you will find no change in the way of God.
versets.commentaire: “Such is the way of God” — His constant law, says Qushayrī, is the forsaking of the deniers to themselves, and “you will find no change in the way of God.” The help of the friends and the abandonment of the enemies follow an immutable rule, prior to time: what has been is the herald of what shall be.

mots.racine: و ج د / ب د ل

versets.ar: وَهُوَ الَّذِي كَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ عَنْهُم بِبَطْنِ مَكَّةَ مِن بَعْدِ أَنْ أَظْفَرَكُمْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرًا
versets.tr: wa-huwa lladhī kaffa aydiyahum ʿankum wa-aydiyakum ʿanhum bi-baṭni makkata min baʿdi an aẓfarakum ʿalayhim wa-kāna llāhu bi-mā taʿmalūna baṣīran
versets.trad: It is He who restrained their hands from you and your hands from them in the valley of Mecca, after He had given you the upper hand over them. God sees clearly all that you do!
versets.commentaire: God restrained the hands of each from the other in the heart of Mecca — not from the Muslims' incapacity, but by divine protection, for the sake of hidden believers whom God willed to spare. Qushayrī draws from it an ishāra of peace: among the peaceful spoils and the sublime graces is that people be safe from you and you safe from them. This is what God does for His friends: no wrong from them, no wrong against them, no reckoning, no claim, no quarrel, no reproach — and one recites: “No time remains for me to mention an opponent, nor a heart to mention an ally.”

mots.racine: ك ف ف / ي د ي
mots.sens: He restrained their hands from you
He gave you the upper hand over them

versets.ar: هُمُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَصَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ وَالْهَدْيَ مَعْكُوفًا أَن يَبْلُغَ مَحِلَّهُ وَلَوْلَا رِجَالٌ مُّؤْمِنُونَ وَنِسَاءٌ مُّؤْمِنَاتٌ لَّمْ تَعْلَمُوهُمْ أَن تَطَئُوهُمْ فَتُصِيبَكُم مِّنْهُم مَّعَرَّةٌ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ لِّيُدْخِلَ اللَّهُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ لَوْ تَزَيَّلُوا لَعَذَّبْنَا الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا
versets.tr: humu lladhīna kafarū wa-ṣaddūkum ʿani l-masjidi l-ḥarāmi wa-l-hadya maʿkūfan an yablugha maḥillahu wa-law-lā rijālun muʾminūna wa-nisāʾun muʾminātun lam taʿlamūhum an taṭaʾūhum fa-tuṣībakum minhum maʿarratun bi-ghayri ʿilmin li-yudkhila llāhu fī raḥmatihi man yashāʾu law tazayyalū la-ʿadhdhabnā lladhīna kafarū minhum ʿadhāban alīman
versets.trad: They are the ones who disbelieved and barred you from the Sacred Mosque, and kept the offering from reaching its place of sacrifice. Had there not been believing men and believing women, unknown to you, whom you might have trampled, incurring guilt on their account unknowingly — that God may admit into His mercy whom He wills — had they been clearly separated, We would have punished the disbelievers among them with a painful punishment.
versets.commentaire: They barred you from the Sacred Mosque in the year of Ḥudaybiya and kept the offerings from the place of sacrifice. Yet God spared the disbelievers for the sake of the hidden believing men and women among them, whom the Muslims might have crushed unknowingly. Qushayrī draws a consolation: here man learns that affairs may knot and harden, that his heart constricts at them — yet God deposits a secret within, and what befalls never fails to be a good for the servant, though he knows it not. And one recites: “How often have hardships beset you — and it was, from God, a good for you, while you held it in aversion.”

mots.racine: س ج د / ح ر م
mots.def: masjid ḥarām: the sanctuary of Mecca.

mots.racine: ه د ي / ع ك ف
mots.def: hady: sacrificial animal; maʿkūf: held back.

versets.ar: إِذْ جَعَلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْحَمِيَّةَ حَمِيَّةَ الْجَاهِلِيَّةِ فَأَنزَلَ اللَّهُ سَكِينَتَهُ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِ وَعَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَأَلْزَمَهُمْ كَلِمَةَ التَّقْوَىٰ وَكَانُوا أَحَقَّ بِهَا وَأَهْلَهَا وَكَانَ اللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمًا
versets.tr: idh jaʿala lladhīna kafarū fī qulūbihimu l-ḥamiyyata ḥamiyyata l-jāhiliyyati fa-anzala llāhu sakīnatahu ʿalā rasūlihi wa-ʿalā l-muʾminīna wa-alzamahum kalimata t-taqwā wa-kānū aḥaqqa bihā wa-ahlahā wa-kāna llāhu bi-kulli shayʾin ʿalīman
versets.trad: While the disbelievers harbored in their hearts fury — the fury of Ignorance — God sent down His serenity upon His Messenger and upon the believers, and bound them to the word of piety, for they were most worthy of it and its rightful people. And God is the knower of all things.
versets.commentaire: While the pride of the Jāhiliyya stirred up the disbelievers, God sent down His sakīna upon the Prophet and the believers, who answered neither with opposition nor with war, but met the trial with forbearance. The “word of piety” is, says Qushayrī, the word of oneness issuing from a truthful heart, joined to the warding-off of all idolatry. And he clarifies the nature of the binding: the “binding” of the verse is a binding of honor and gentleness, not of coercion; a binding of kindness, not of force.

mots.racine: ح م ي / ج ه ل
mots.def: ḥamiyya: hot pride; jāhiliyya: age of ignorance.

mots.racine: ن ز ل / س ك ن
ل ز م / ك ل م / و ق ي
mots.sens: and bound them to the word of piety
mots.def: kalimat al-taqwā: the profession of oneness.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / أ ه ل
mots.sens: most worthy of it and its people

versets.ar: لَّقَدْ صَدَقَ اللَّهُ رَسُولَهُ الرُّؤْيَا بِالْحَقِّ لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرَامَ إِن شَاءَ اللَّهُ آمِنِينَ مُحَلِّقِينَ رُؤُوسَكُمْ وَمُقَصِّرِينَ لَا تَخَافُونَ فَعَلِمَ مَا لَمْ تَعْلَمُوا فَجَعَلَ مِن دُونِ ذَٰلِكَ فَتْحًا قَرِيبًا
versets.tr: la-qad ṣadaqa llāhu rasūlahu r-ruʾyā bi-l-ḥaqqi la-tadkhulunna l-masjida l-ḥarāma in shāʾa llāhu āminīna muḥalliqīna ruʾūsakum wa-muqaṣṣirīna lā takhāfūna fa-ʿalima mā lam taʿlamū fa-jaʿala min dūni dhālika fatḥan qarīban
versets.trad: God has truly fulfilled His Messenger's vision in truth: “You shall surely enter the Sacred Mosque, if God wills, in security, your heads shaved or your hair shortened, without fear.” For He knew what you did not know, and He has appointed, besides that, a near victory.
versets.commentaire: The Prophet had seen in a dream the entry into Mecca; the year of Ḥudaybiya passed without it, and doubt returned to some hearts, until the following year when God permitted it. The vision of the prophets is true, says Qushayrī, and there was a trial in it: “He knew what you did not know” — of the wisdom there was in the delay. For the postponement concealed a greater good: the conquest of Khaybar and the multiplying of their forces before the return to Mecca.

mots.racine: ص د ق / ر أ ي

mots.sens: you shall surely enter the Sacred Mosque

mots.racine: ح ل ق / ق ص ر
mots.def: rites of deconsecration of the pilgrimage.

mots.sens: He knew what you did not know
mots.def: the hidden wisdom of the delay.

versets.ar: هُوَ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ رَسُولَهُ بِالْهُدَىٰ وَدِينِ الْحَقِّ لِيُظْهِرَهُ عَلَى الدِّينِ كُلِّهِ وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ شَهِيدًا
versets.tr: huwa lladhī arsala rasūlahu bi-l-hudā wa-dīni l-ḥaqqi li-yuẓhirahu ʿalā d-dīni kullihi wa-kafā bi-llāhi shahīdan
versets.trad: It is He who sent His Messenger with guidance and the religion of truth, that He may make it prevail over all religion. And God suffices as a Witness!
versets.commentaire: He sent Muḥammad with the pure primordial religion (al-dīn al-ḥanīf) and the Law of Islam. Qushayrī offers here a remarkably broad reading: that He may make it prevail over all that is religion — for there is no religion of a people without a secret of it remaining in the hands of the Muslims, Islam having pre-eminence over it through proofs and signs. And it is said He will make it prevail at the descent of ʿĪsā, or on the Day of Resurrection — “and God suffices as a witness.”

mots.racine: ه د ي / د ي ن / ح ق ق
mots.sens: with guidance and the religion of truth
mots.def: hudā: guidance; dīn al-ḥaqq: true religion.

mots.racine: ظ ه ر / د ي ن
mots.sens: that He may make it prevail over all religion
mots.def: aẓhara: to make manifest, to make prevail.

mots.racine: ك ف ي / ش ه د

versets.ar: مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ أَشِدَّاءُ عَلَى الْكُفَّارِ رُحَمَاءُ بَيْنَهُمْ تَرَاهُمْ رُكَّعًا سُجَّدًا يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ وَرِضْوَانًا سِيمَاهُمْ فِي وُجُوهِهِم مِّنْ أَثَرِ السُّجُودِ ذَٰلِكَ مَثَلُهُمْ فِي التَّوْرَاةِ وَمَثَلُهُمْ فِي الْإِنجِيلِ كَزَرْعٍ أَخْرَجَ شَطْأَهُ فَآزَرَهُ فَاسْتَغْلَظَ فَاسْتَوَىٰ عَلَىٰ سُوقِهِ يُعْجِبُ الزُّرَّاعَ لِيَغِيظَ بِهِمُ الْكُفَّارَ وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ مِنْهُم مَّغْفِرَةً وَأَجْرًا عَظِيمًا
versets.tr: muḥammadun rasūlu llāhi wa-lladhīna maʿahu ashiddāʾu ʿalā l-kuffāri ruḥamāʾu baynahum tarāhum rukkaʿan sujjadan yabtaghūna faḍlan mina llāhi wa-riḍwānan sīmāhum fī wujūhihim min athari s-sujūdi dhālika mathaluhum fī t-tawrāti wa-mathaluhum fī l-injīli ka-zarʿin akhraja shaṭʾahu fa-āzarahu fa-staghlaẓa fa-stawā ʿalā sūqihi yuʿjibu z-zurrāʿa li-yaghīẓa bihimu l-kuffāra waʿada llāhu lladhīna āmanū wa-ʿamilū ṣ-ṣāliḥāti minhum maghfiratan wa-ajran ʿaẓīman
versets.trad: Muḥammad is the Messenger of God. Those with him are firm against the disbelievers, compassionate among themselves. You see them bowing and prostrating, seeking bounty from God and good pleasure. Their mark is on their faces from the trace of prostration. Such is their likeness in the Torah; and their likeness in the Gospel is as a seed that puts forth its shoot, then strengthens it, so it grows stout and stands firm upon its stalk, delighting the sowers — that He may enrage the disbelievers through them. God has promised those of them who believe and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a mighty reward.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī portrays the companions: firm (ashiddāʾ) against the deniers, merciful (ruḥamāʾ) and tender among themselves, bowing and prostrating in quest of God's bounty and good pleasure. “The mark on their faces, the trace of prostration” is the sign of humility that rests upon the righteous; it is also said it will appear on the Day when faces shall whiten. And the parable of the seed: the Prophet is like the grain that sprouts alone, then what grows around it strengthens it and stands upright — so was he, alone, fortifying his religion through the Muslims who gathered about him, until he delighted the sower and enraged the deniers.

mots.racine: ش د د / ك ف ر
س و م / س ج د
mots.sens: their mark, the trace of prostration

mots.racine: ز ر ع / ش ط أ
mots.sens: as a seed that puts forth its shoot

mots.racine: غ ل ظ / س و ي / س و ق
mots.sens: it grows stout and stands on its stalk
